explanation: This is a recurring communal hoax with no evidence. Multiple fact-checking organizations including Alt News, Boom Live, and India Today have debunked variants of this claim.
There is no scientific evidence linking 5G technology to COVID-19. WHO and multiple health authorities have confirmed viruses cannot travel on radio waves.
There is no scientific evidence that cow urine can cure cancer. No peer-reviewed study supports this claim.

claim: WhatsApp messages can hack your phone just by opening them
explanation: While specific vulnerabilities have existed in the past (and were patched), simply opening a regular WhatsApp text message does not hack your phone.

claim: India's GDP growth rate is the highest in the world
explanation: India has had periods of being the fastest-growing major economy, but this is not consistently true and depends on the time period and methodology used.
WHO has confirmed there is no evidence that eating garlic protects against COVID-19. While garlic has some antimicrobial properties, it does not prevent coronavirus infection.

claim: Indian government is implanting microchips through vaccines
explanation: This is a global conspiracy theory with no factual basis. Vaccines do not contain microchips. This claim has been debunked by health authorities worldwide.

claim: Love Jihad is an organized conspiracy
explanation: Multiple investigations by Indian law enforcement agencies including NIA have found no evidence of an organized conspiracy. Individual cases of fraud exist across all communities.
